name: Uses & Gratifications Theory (Katz, Blumler, Ruggiero)
core_claim: Uses & Gratifications (U&G) inverts the older 'what does media do to people' question into 'what do people do with media'. Its central claim is that audiences are active, goal-directed agents who select and consume media to satisfy pre-existing psychological and social NEEDS — and that the same content can gratify different needs for different people. Enjoyment/engagement is therefore not a property of the work but the degree of fit between the gratifications a person seeks (GS) and the gratifications they obtain (GO) from a given work. Katz, Gurevitch & Haas (1973) ground this in a need typology; Blumler & Katz (1974) frame the active audience; Ruggiero (2000) revives the model for interactive/computer-mediated media, arguing the affordances of new media (interactivity, demassification, asynchronicity) make U&G more relevant, not less. The driver of engagement is need-satisfaction, with the unit of explanation being the viewer's motive, not the text's content.
descriptive_vs_experiential: U&G operates primarily at the MOTIVATIONAL/needs level — neither a description of what a story is ABOUT (topical) nor purely what it FEELS LIKE (affective texture), but WHY a viewer reaches for it. It sits one layer behind both: a topical descriptor (e.g. 'crime') and an experiential descriptor (e.g. 'tense') are both means to a gratification end (e.g. escapism, surveillance, mood management). It is closest to a functional/teleological framework: it classifies media use by the need served. Affective/experiential dimensions appear only instrumentally (e.g. the 'affective needs' cluster — emotion, pleasure — and Zillmann-derived mood management), and topical content appears only as a vehicle for a gratification. This makes it orthogonal to a content taxonomy and complementary to one: it asks the 'so that...' question that themes and moods do not answer.
key_dimensions: Two main typologies. (1) Katz, Gurevitch & Haas (1973) — 35 needs in five clusters: COGNITIVE needs (information, knowledge, understanding, surveillance of the environment); AFFECTIVE needs (emotional experience, aesthetic/pleasurable experience, feeling); PERSONAL INTEGRATIVE needs (credibility, confidence, stability, status, self-esteem, reassurance); SOCIAL INTEGRATIVE needs (strengthening contact with family, friends, the world — connection, belonging); TENSION-RELEASE / ESCAPIST needs (escape, diversion, relaxation, distraction from problems). (2) The classic Blumler/McQuail/Katz media-use categories often summarised as four: DIVERSION (escape, emotional release), PERSONAL RELATIONSHIPS (companionship, social utility/'water-cooler' currency), PERSONAL IDENTITY (self-reference, value reinforcement, models of behaviour), and SURVEILLANCE (information about the world). Ruggiero (2000) adds new-media-specific gratifications: INTERACTIVITY, DEMASSIFICATION (personal control over selection), and ASYNCHRONICITY. Later contemporary syntheses commonly distil to: information, entertainment, escapism/diversion, social interaction/connection, personal identity, mood management.
cross_cultural_validity: Mixed. The foundational Katz, Gurevitch & Haas (1973) study used an ISRAELI survey sample, so the model was non-US from inception — a point in its favour against an English/American-centric charge. The need typology has been replicated across many national contexts (US, UK, Israel, China, India, and across digital platforms globally), which gives it broad face-validity. However, the framework is repeatedly criticised as INDIVIDUALISTIC (Ien Ang; Elliott 1974) — it models the atomised viewer seeking personal gratification and under-weights the social/collective context of viewing. This is a real cross-cultural fault line: 'social integrative' and 'personal identity' gratifications are likely weighted and expressed differently in collectivist vs individualist cultures, and the salience of 'escape' vs 'surveillance' shifts with media ecology and political environment. The CLUSTERS appear to travel; the relative WEIGHTS and the meaning of identity/social gratifications do not travel cleanly. [uncertain] — there is no single authoritative cross-cultural invariance study quantifying which clusters are stable.
empirical_grounding: Substantial but methodologically contested. The model is overwhelmingly validated through SELF-REPORT SURVEY data — factor-analytic studies that extract gratification dimensions from questionnaire batteries, and correlational studies linking gratifications-sought to media-choice and satisfaction. This is its strongest evidence base (decades of replication across media) and its weakest point: critics (Elliott 1974; Lichtenstein & Rosenfeld 1983) call it 'mentalistic', circular (gratifications are often artefacts of researcher-supplied items rather than subject-generated), and atheoretical. It is rarely validated against BEHAVIOURAL completion/engagement data — the GS/GO (sought vs obtained) distinction is the closest it comes to a predictive, falsifiable mechanism, and there is real correlational support that GS-GO discrepancy predicts satisfaction and continued use. There is little experimental causal evidence. To validate LLM-extracted dimensions against U&G you would need to (a) infer which gratifications a work AFFORDS from its synopsis, then (b) correlate those affordances against panel self-reported sought-gratifications + completion/rewatch data — i.e. a join of content-side extraction with viewer-side survey, which U&G alone does not supply.
operationalisability: Partial and lossy. U&G's core variable — the VIEWER's sought gratification — is NOT extractable from a synopsis at all; it lives in the person, not the text. What an LLM CAN extract from a synopsis is the gratification a work plausibly AFFORDS: a light ensemble comedy affords diversion/relaxation; a true-crime documentary affords surveillance/cognitive needs; a tear-jerker affords affective/eudaimonic gratification; a cult sci-fi affords social-currency ('have you seen it') and identity gratifications. Extraction unit is the WHOLE WORK (gratification affordance is a gestalt property, not scene-level). Failure modes: (1) conflating affordance with viewer need — the synopsis cannot say WHO will seek what; (2) escapism and mood-management are mood-dependent and invisible in text; (3) social-currency/'buzz' gratification depends on cultural moment and popularity data, not synopsis content; (4) personal-identity gratification (value reinforcement, self-reference) is intrinsically relational and cannot be read off the work alone; (5) the same dark drama affords both 'escape' (for one viewer) and 'confront-my-feelings' eudaimonic appreciation (for another) — the LLM cannot disambiguate without the viewer.
motivation_served: U&G is the framework that most directly enumerates VIEWER MOTIVATIONS, so it maps to nearly all of them simultaneously: hedonic enjoyment (diversion, relaxation, tension-release — the escapist cluster), eudaimonic appreciation (affective + cognitive needs, being moved/learning), mood regulation (tension-release; aligns with Zillmann's mood management), identity expression (personal-identity/personal-integrative cluster), and social connection (social-integrative cluster, companionship, social currency). For GROUP viewing this is the single most useful lens in the taxonomy: two people choosing together are reconciling DIFFERENT sought-gratifications, not different topics. Person A wants escape/decompression after work; Person B wants stimulation/surveillance. The joint-watch problem is fundamentally a gratification-negotiation problem. A recommender that models each viewer's dominant sought-gratifications can find works whose AFFORDANCE PROFILE overlaps both — e.g. a propulsive thriller that affords both A's escapism and B's cognitive engagement. This reframes 'shared taste' as 'overlapping gratification affordance', which is the more tractable target.
receiver_dependence: Strongly receiver-dependent — this is the framework's defining property and its key contribution to a two-person recommender. The gratification SOUGHT is wholly a property of the viewer (their current mood, social situation, identity, life stage). The gratification a work AFFORDS is a property of the work, but it is a DISPOSITIONAL property (a capacity to satisfy a need under the right viewer state), not an absolute one like gore-level or runtime. Critically, the SAME work affords different gratifications to different viewers and to the same viewer in different moods. So the U&G axes are almost entirely RELATIONAL, not absolute: 'escapist' is not a fixed label on a show, it is a fit between a show's low-cognitive-load affordance and a viewer in a depleted state. For HelpME2C this means U&G dimensions should be modelled as work-affordance vectors that get scored AGAINST per-viewer (and per-session-mood) sought-gratification vectors — never as static tags that determine recommendation alone.
blind_spots: (1) Active-audience overreach — it assumes viewers consciously know and can articulate their needs; much real viewing is habitual, passive, or algorithmically nudged, which U&G under-models. (2) It is content-blind: it tells you WHY someone watches but says nothing about WHAT distinguishes one escapist show from another — it cannot, alone, power discovery within a gratification bucket. (3) The social context of co-viewing is famously under-theorised (Ien Ang) — ironic given it is the most group-relevant framework. (4) Circularity/measurement dependence — gratifications are often artefacts of the survey instrument. (5) It cannot capture craft/quality, aesthetic distinctiveness, novelty, or the 'good-bad' axis of appeal. (6) Negative/ambivalent engagement (hate-watching, doomscrolling, compulsion) fits awkwardly. (7) Mood-dependent and session-dependent gratifications are invisible to any static profile. (8) It says nothing about narrative structure, theme, or the experiential texture two viewers might bond over.
vocabulary_implications: U&G should NOT be encoded as content theme slugs — it operates one layer behind themes and belongs in a SEPARATE FACET: a 'gratification-affordance' axis distinct from the narrative-theme vocabulary. Proposed controlled vocabulary for an LLM-extractable work-affordance facet (whole-work level, multi-label, scored 0-1): `affords-escapism` (low-cognitive-load comfort/diversion), `affords-mood-lift` (uplifting/feel-good), `affords-catharsis` (emotional release, tear-jerker), `affords-stimulation` (intellectually engaging, puzzle/surveillance/learning), `affords-social-currency` (buzzy/'must-discuss'/zeitgeist), `affords-identity-resonance` (value-reinforcing, self-reference — FLAG: relational, weakly extractable), `affords-companionship` (cosy, low-stakes background-watchable), `affords-awe-or-meaning` (eudaimonic, being-moved). Crucially: these are AFFORDANCES, not viewer needs. The viewer-side sought-gratification vector must come from a DIFFERENT data source (onboarding mood/context questions, watch-history inference, session-time signals) and is NOT extractable from any synopsis. `affords-social-currency` additionally requires popularity/recency data, not synopsis text. Recommend: add the affordance facet; keep it orthogonal to the theme taxonomy; build the per-viewer sought-gratification side as a separate model input feeding the group reconciliation step.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: U&G is largely ORTHOGONAL to the existing slug set — the existing vocabulary is overwhelmingly descriptive/topical (what a work is about) and U&G is motivational (why someone watches). It does not validate or invalidate individual theme slugs so much as reveal a MISSING FACET. Mappings/tensions: (a) The Texture cluster (`slow-burn`, `dark-comedy`, `absurdist-comedy`, `character-study`, `meditation-on-death`) is the closest existing analogue to gratification affordance and is where U&G most overlaps — `dark-comedy`/`absurdist-comedy` lean diversion/mood-management; `meditation-on-time`/`meditation-on-death` lean eudaimonic/cognitive. (b) Genre/structure slugs (`mystery-investigation`, `whodunit`, `heist-and-caper`) are strong proxies for the surveillance/cognitive-stimulation gratification. (c) `found-family`, `friendship-tested`, `platonic-soulmates` proxy social-integrative/companionship gratification. (d) Identity/self slugs (`identity-crisis`, `coming-out`, `finding-purpose`) proxy the personal-identity gratification but as TOPIC not as the relational resonance U&G means. GAP REVEALED: the vocabulary has no way to express 'this is a decompression/comfort watch' vs 'this is a demanding watch' — i.e. cognitive-load and gratification-type are entirely absent as a facet. This is the single most actionable insight for group recommendation: add a gratification-affordance facet alongside, not inside, the theme list.
key_sources: Katz, E., Gurevitch, M. & Haas, H. (1973). On the Use of the Mass Media for Important Things. American Sociological Review 38, 164-181 — freely accessible at https://repository.upenn.edu/asc_papers/267/ . Blumler, J.G. & Katz, E. (eds.) (1974). The Uses of Mass Communications — book, library/paywalled. Ruggiero, T.E. (2000). Uses and Gratifications Theory in the 21st Century. Mass Communication & Society 3(1), 3-37 — open PDF at http://kayesweetser.com/wp-content/uploads/2007/08/adpr5990_ruggerio.pdf ; publisher (paywalled) https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1207/S15327825MCS0301_02 . Lichtenstein, A. & Rosenfeld, L.B. (1983). Uses and Misuses of Gratifications Research. Communication Research 10(1) — paywalled https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/009365083010001005 . Overview (free): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uses_and_gratifications_theory . Related: Zillmann mood management theory (mood-regulation gratification).
